cycles.name: Foundation Forge
focuses: Build foundational strength across lower body, upper body, and core with corrective priorities.
Establish movement patterns that counteract tennis-specific postural imbalances.
Develop scapular stability and posterior chain strength as injury-prevention anchors.
Introduce reactive plyometric work to build tendon elasticity and court-transfer qualities.

cycles.name: Strength Engine
focuses: Progressive strength overload across all major patterns with knee and shoulder injury management.
Intensify scapular stability and posterior chain work as injury-prevention anchors.

paragraphs: Mehraneh, Cycle 2 is where the foundation gets tested. The patterns you built in the first five weeks now carry real load — and your body is ready for it. This cycle is designed around one core truth: you cannot build the athletic armor you need while managing active injuries if you try to push through pain. Every programming decision this block respects what your knees and shoulder are telling you, while still driving genuine strength adaptation everywhere else.

Mentally, this cycle asks you to stay disciplined under two pressures at once — higher training intensity and the instinct to either push too hard or pull back too much. Trust the RPE numbers. They are your guide, not your limit. On the days your stress is high and your sleep was poor, the program still works — just execute it precisely and don't add to it.

By the end of Cycle 2, expect measurably stronger pulling strength, improved rotational power through medicine ball work, reduced upper back discomfort from the intensified shoulder integrity work, and a noticeable improvement in single-leg stability under load.
outcomes: Measurably stronger pulling strength across rows and lat pull-down
Improved rotational power through medicine ball work
Reduced upper back discomfort from intensified shoulder integrity work
Noticeable improvement in single-leg stability under load
Knee pain managed and not worsened — pain-free range expanded

cycles.name: Power Transfer
focuses: Convert built strength into court-relevant power — rotational, reactive, and unilateral.
Reinforce shoulder girdle resilience under higher velocity demands.

teaser.subtitle: Cycle 3 is where strength becomes something the opponent can feel.
paragraphs: Everything you've built — the pulling strength, the hip stability, the rotational foundation — gets converted into court speed, explosive first steps, and power that transfers directly into your game. The work gets faster, more reactive, and more specific to what tennis actually demands from your body.
Earn it in these five weeks.

cycles.name: Performance Peak
focuses: Sharpen athletic output — speed, reactive strength, and sport-specific conditioning.
Sustain structural integrity under competition load; refine movement economy.

cycles.name: Armor & Maintain
focuses: Maintain peak strength while reducing injury risk heading into high-competition periods.
Optimize recovery capacity and tissue resilience for the long season.

days.completionMessage: Strong session — knees respected, foundation loaded.

good: Both sit bones stay heavy — rotate the spine, not the pelvis
bad: Don't compensate limited range by tilting the pelvis

good: Sit back and down like lowering onto a low chair
bad: Don't allow knees to drift inward on the descent

good: Drive the knee forward directly over the pinky toe
bad: Don't let the heel rise — range comes from the ankle, not the foot

good: Push the floor apart under your feet — screw them into the ground
Feel the glutes load at the bottom before you drive up
bad: Don't chase depth if it provokes knee pain — stop at pain-free range

good: Keep the torso upright — like a flagpole through the spine
Feel the front glute doing the work, not the quad pushing off the back foot
bad: Don't let the front knee translate forward aggressively — drive through the heel

good: Sit between your heels — chest stays tall throughout
Feel both knees tracking consistently over the toes without pain
bad: Don't use this as a heavily loaded squat — keep weight moderate, this is a controlled range builder

good: Roll the ball in with both heels, then lower slowly — the descent is the stimulus
Feel the hamstrings resisting the extension on the way out
bad: Don't let the hips drop — keep the glutes squeezed and hips bridged throughout

exercises.name: Standing Dumbbell Single Leg Calf Raise

good: Rise through the big toe — full elevation at the top
Feel the Achilles tendon load under control — no bouncing
bad: Don't rush the bottom position; own the full stretch on every rep

good: Imagine someone trying to rotate your hips — deny them completely
Feel the obliques and deep core firing to resist the cable
bad: Don't let the torso lean away from the cable to compensate

good: Lower the limbs slowly — like writing your name in the air with precision
Press the lower back into the floor throughout — never lose that contact
bad: Don't let the lower back arch as the opposite arm and leg extend

days.completionMessage: Shoulder integrity work done — this is the most important session of the week.

good: Pull through the rear delts — the hands travel in a wide arc
Feel the squeeze between the shoulder blades at full range
bad: Don't shrug; keep the neck long and the traps quiet

good: Keep the back of the wrists and elbows in contact with the wall throughout
Feel the lower traps and serratus engaging as the arms rise
bad: Don't let the lower back arch away from the wall to reach range

good: The forearm rotates like a door opening — nothing else moves
Feel the posterior rotator cuff working — not the bicep or delt
bad: Don't let the elbow drift away from the side

good: Drive the elbows back and down — like tucking them into your back pockets
Feel the mid-back doing the work, not the biceps pulling
bad: Don't jerk at the top — control is the whole point of chest support

good: Pull the bar to the upper chest — elbows travel down and back
Feel the lats engaging from the armpit down — not the biceps
bad: Don't lean back excessively to initiate — keep the torso relatively upright

good: Press in a slight arc — not straight up; follow the natural shoulder path
Feel the scapula move with the press — not pinned and rigid
bad: Don't force range on the right side; stop before any impingement sensation

exercises.name: Half Kneeling Single Arm Cable Row

good: Keep the hips square — the pull comes from the back, not a hip shift
Feel the core bracing against the rotation the cable creates
bad: Don't use torso rotation to assist the pull

good: Slow and deliberate — this is precision work, not a strength exercise
Feel the posterior cuff controlling the movement throughout the arc
bad: Don't go heavy; if you can't control it with perfect form, reduce load

good: Each position targets a different upper back zone — own each one
Feel the lower and mid traps loading as the arms form each letter
bad: Don't shrug into the movement — keep the neck long throughout

good: Pull the handles to your ears — elbows high and wide at the finish
Feel the rear delts and external rotators squeezing at the end position
bad: Don't let the body swing forward to assist — maintain the lean throughout

days.completionMessage: Rotational power work complete — that's court-specific strength.

good: Hips stay level — imagine a full cup of water on your lower back
Feel the glute of the standing leg firing to maintain position
bad: Don't let the free hip drop below the standing hip

good: Slow, controlled extension — limbs move like they're under water
Press the lower back firmly into the floor throughout
bad: Don't let the lumbar arch break contact with the floor

good: Keep the band taut throughout — never let it go slack
Feel the glute med firing on the stance leg as you step
bad: Don't lean the torso to assist the step — stay upright

good: Drive from the hips — the arms follow the rotation, they don't lead it
Feel the obliques and hip loading before the arms release
bad: Don't use just the arms; the power comes from the ground up

good: Throw through the wall, not at it — maximum intent on each rep
Feel the hip extend and rotate before the arms release
bad: Don't stand square; load the back hip first like a coiled spring

good: Drive the knee up explosively at the top — like sprinting in place
Feel the full glute of the working leg extending at the top
bad: Don't push off the back foot to assist — all force from the front leg

good: Push the floor away — the bar rises as a consequence of your leg drive
Feel the lats bracing against the bar path, keeping the spine neutral
bad: Don't let the lower back round at the initiation — hinge, don't squat

good: Hinge like a door — the spine and free leg form one long lever
Feel the hamstring of the working leg loading as you descend
bad: Don't let the pelvis rotate open — keep both hip points square to the floor

good: Walk tall — crown of head reaching the ceiling, shoulders packed
Feel the core bracing 360° around the spine with every step
bad: Don't let the shoulders creep toward the ears as fatigue builds

days.completionMessage: Zone 2 and mobility complete — this session is doing more for you than you think.

good: Conversational pace — you should be able to speak in full sentences
Feel the breathing settle into a steady, sustainable rhythm
bad: Don't let heart rate creep above 140; reduce intensity immediately if it does

good: Open the chest fully toward the ceiling on the rotation
Feel each segment of the thoracic spine unlocking with each rep
bad: Don't rush; spend a full breath in each end position

good: Both sit bones stay grounded — rotate, don't rock
Feel the hip capsule opening on each directional shift
bad: Don't compensate limited range by letting the pelvis tilt

good: Follow the elbow with the eyes — maximize the arc
Feel the upper back separating from the lower back in the rotation
bad: Don't let the hips rotate to assist — movement is thoracic only

good: Walk the hands as far as possible to each side
Feel the lats and the side of the thorax opening under the stretch
bad: Don't sit back off the heels — maintain the hip position throughout

good: Stand tall in the stretch — don't collapse the chest to increase range
Feel the anterior shoulder and pec opening, not straining
bad: Don't force the shoulder into impingement range — respect the right side

good: Wave through the spine — tail to crown on the arch, crown to tail on the curl
Feel each vertebra participating individually
bad: Don't move only at the lumbar — distribute the motion through the whole spine

exercises.name: Arm Bike / Ski Erg / Cross Trainer

cards.title: Knee Pain Protocol
cards.body: Both knees are currently at 5/10 pain — this is active, not historical. All squatting movements have been modified: depth is pain-free range only, plyometric impact has been removed entirely from this cycle, and eccentric loading (stability ball leg curl, heel-elevated goblet squat) is used deliberately to build tendon and quad resilience. If pain rises above 5/10 during any movement, stop and substitute with the leg press or leg extension at the same tempo. Do not push through knee pain.

cards.title: Right Shoulder — Manage, Don't Aggravate
cards.body: Your right shoulder has been painful for over a year. This cycle doubles the corrective volume: three dedicated shoulder integrity exercises every upper body day. Pressing on the right side is intentionally load-conservative — never exceed RPE 6 on right-side pressing. The Y-T-W, face pull, and external rotation work are the priority, not the strength movements. If the shoulder flares above 4/10 during pressing, drop to bodyweight or band-only and maintain the pulling and corrective work.

cards.title: Stress at 10 — This Changes Everything
cards.body: A stress score of 10 means your nervous system is operating at its ceiling before training even begins. RPE targets in this program are set conservatively on purpose — do not exceed them on high-stress days. On days when you feel overwhelmed or exhausted, the conditioning and mobility session is always the right choice over a heavy strength session. Adaptation happens in recovery, and you cannot outwork a chronically stressed nervous system.

cards.title: Sleep Quality Is Dropping — Protect It
cards.body: Sleep quality at 6 with stress at 10 is a concerning combination at your training volume. Prioritize sleep onset: consistent bedtime, cool room, no screens for 30 minutes before bed. The Zone 2 conditioning session will actively improve sleep quality over time — treat it as a recovery investment, not a training session. If sleep drops below 6 hours for two or more consecutive nights, reduce Day 1 and Day 3 loads by one RPE point until it recovers.

cards.title: Fuel the Work — Especially Around Sessions
cards.body: At 55kg with active tennis, gym training, and high stress, insufficient caloric intake will prevent any muscle development and slow injury recovery significantly. You don't need to count every calorie — but aim for protein at every meal and eat a carbohydrate source within 60 minutes of finishing each session. The post-session window is when your muscles are most receptive to recovery nutrition. Skipping it is leaving adaptation on the table.

cards.title: Power Without Impact
cards.body: High-impact plyometrics have been removed this cycle due to active knee pain. Medicine ball rotational work replaces them as the primary power development tool — and for a tennis player, this is arguably more specific anyway. Every throw and slam is training the exact rotational force production pattern your game demands. Focus on maximum intent on every rep — a half-hearted throw is wasted training.
